era_context: The late 8th century continues under Charlemagne's consolidation of Frankish power and the early reign of Harun al-Rashid in the Abbasid Caliphate. The Byzantine Empire under Irene (regent for Constantine VI, r. 780–797) is in a period of internal political tension. In East Asia, the Tang Dynasty maintains cultural and administrative stability, while Japan's Nara period is in its final years before the transition to Heian.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern and Eastern Europe beyond Frankish influence (sparse sources)
Central Asia (limited sources; primarily known through Chinese and Islamic records)

events.title: Charlemagne issues the Admonitio Generalis (General Admonition)
events.description: Charlemagne issues a comprehensive capitulary (legislative decree) known as the Admonitio Generalis, addressing ecclesiastical reform, moral conduct, and administrative standardization across the Frankish realm. This document reflects the Carolingian Renaissance's emphasis on education, correct liturgical practice, and the integration of church and state authority. The Admonitio Generalis is one of the most important legislative documents of the early medieval period and establishes standards for clerical training and monastic discipline.
key_figures: Alcuin of York (scholar and advisor, c. 735–804)

sources.name: Capitularia Regum Francorum (Capitularies of the Frankish Kings)

events.certainty_note: The Admonitio Generalis survives in multiple manuscript copies and is dated to 789 in contemporary sources. The document's authenticity and Charlemagne's authorship are not disputed.

events.title: Byzantine-Abbasid naval conflict in the Mediterranean
events.description: Naval skirmishes and raids occur along the Byzantine-Abbasid maritime frontier in the Mediterranean and Aegean seas. These conflicts are part of the ongoing Byzantine-Arab Wars (633–1050s) and reflect competition for control of Mediterranean trade routes and coastal territories. The specific engagements of 789 are less well-documented than major campaigns, but naval raiding and counter-raiding are consistent features of this period.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: Byzantine-Abbasid naval conflicts are well-attested for this period, but specific engagements in 789 are not detailed in surviving sources. The event is inferred from the broader pattern of Mediterranean warfare during this era.
cross_references: Contemporary with Harun al-Rashid's military expansion

events.title: Council of Frankfurt convened by Charlemagne
events.description: Charlemagne convenes a major ecclesiastical council at Frankfurt to address theological disputes, particularly regarding the veneration of images (icons). The council rejects both the iconoclasm of the Byzantine East and the extreme icon veneration endorsed at the Second Council of Nicaea (787). This represents a distinctly Frankish theological position and reinforces Charlemagne's role as arbiter of religious orthodoxy in Western Europe.

sources.name: Libri Carolini (Caroline Books) - theological response to Nicaea II

events.certainty_note: The Council of Frankfurt is documented in contemporary Frankish sources. The council's decisions and theological positions are preserved in the Libri Carolini and conciliar records.

disconfirming_evidence: The Admonitio Generalis, while authentic, may not have been as universally implemented as Charlemagne's court records suggest. Modern scholarship indicates that compliance with Carolingian reforms was uneven across the realm, and many monasteries and dioceses continued pre-reform practices. The Council of Frankfurt's theological position on icons, presented as a definitive Frankish stance, was not universally accepted even within the Frankish church and was later superseded by different interpretations. The extent of Byzantine-Abbasid naval conflict in 789 specifically is uncertain; sources may conflate events from different years.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 789 is strong for the Frankish Empire, where court records and ecclesiastical sources are abundant. Byzantine and Abbasid sources are later compilations, introducing potential chronological confusion and legendary material. The Council of Frankfurt is well-documented, but its actual implementation and long-term impact are debated by modern scholars. Sources reflect Frankish court perspectives and may overstate the authority and reach of Charlemagne's decrees. Non-European regions remain essentially undocumented in surviving written sources.

graph_edges.note: Admonitio Generalis issued during ongoing Saxon Wars
Council of Frankfurt rejects the icon veneration endorsed at Nicaea II
Naval conflicts continue as part of broader Byzantine-Abbasid competition
